title: Ensure Compute Instances Are Launched With Shielded VM  Enabled

input_row.title: Ensure Compute Instances Are Launched With Shielded VM  Enabled
input_row.description: To defend against advanced threats and ensure that the boot loader and firmware on your VMs are signed and untampered, it is recommended that Compute instances are launched with Shielded VM enabled.
input_row.rationale: Shielded VMs are virtual machines (VMs) on Google Cloud Platform hardened by a set of security controls that help defend against rootkits and bootkits. Shielded VM offers verifiable integrity of your Compute Engine VM instances, so you can be confident your instances haven't been compromised by boot- or kernel-level malware or rootkits. Shielded VM's verifiable integrity is achieved through the use of Secure Boot, virtual trusted platform module (vTPM)-enabled Measured Boot, and integrity monitoring. Shielded VM instances run firmware which is signed and verified using Google's Certificate Authority, ensuring that the instance's firmware is unmodified and establishing the root of trust for Secure Boot. Integrity monitoring helps you understand and make decisions about the state of your VM instances and the Shielded VM vTPM enables Measured Boot by performing the measurements needed to create a known good boot baseline, called the integrity policy baseline. The integrity policy baseline is used for comparison with measurements from subsequent VM boots to determine if anything has changed. Secure Boot helps ensure that the system only runs authentic software by verifying the digital signature of all boot components, and halting the boot process if signature verification fails.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. Click on the instance name to see its VM instance details page. 3. Under the section Shielded VM, ensure that vTPM and Integrity Monitoring are on. From Google Cloud CLI 1. For each instance in your project, get its metadata: gcloud compute instances list --format=json | jq -r '. | "vTPM: \(.[].shieldedInstanceConfig.enableVtpm) IntegrityMonitoring: \(.[].shieldedInstanceConfig.enableIntegrityMonitoring) Name: \(.[].name)"' 2. Ensure that there is a shieldedInstanceConfig configuration and that configuration has the enableIntegrityMonitoring and enableVtpm set to true. If the VM is not a Shield VM image, you will not see a shieldedInstanceConfig` in the output.
input_row.remediation: To be able turn on Shielded VM on an instance, your instance must use an image with Shielded VM support. From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. Click on the instance name to see its VM instance details page. 3. Click STOP to stop the instance. 4. When the instance has stopped, click EDIT. 5. In the Shielded VM section, select Turn on vTPM and Turn on Integrity Monitoring. 6. Optionally, if you do not use any custom or unsigned drivers on the instance, also select Turn on Secure Boot. 7. Click the Save button to modify the instance and then click START to restart it. From Google Cloud CLI You can only enable Shielded VM options on instances that have Shielded VM support. For a list of Shielded VM public images, run the gcloud compute images list command with the following flags: gcloud compute images list --project gce-uefi-images --no-standard-images 1. Stop the instance: gcloud compute instances stop 2. Update the instance: gcloud compute instances update --shielded-vtpm --shielded- vm-integrity-monitoring 3. Optionally, if you do not use any custom or unsigned drivers on the instance, also turn on secure boot. gcloud compute instances update --shielded-vm-secure-boot 4. Restart the instance: gcloud compute instances start Prevention: You can ensure that all new VMs will be created with Shielded VM enabled by setting up an Organization Policy to for Shielded VM at https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/orgpolicies/compute-requireShieldedVm. Learn more at: https://cloud.google.com/security/shielded-cloud/shielded-vm#organization-policy- constraint.
input_row.default_value: By default, Compute Instances do not have Shielded VM enabled.
input_row.additional_information: If you do use custom or unsigned drivers on the instance, enabling Secure Boot will cause the machine to no longer boot. Turn on Secure Boot only on instances that have been verified to not have any custom drivers installed.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the following:

- **GCP API calls or Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) SDK methods:** 
  - Use `gcloud compute instances list` to enumerate all VM instances within the project.
 
- **GCP CLI commands (if applicable):**
  - `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` to get a detailed JSON output of all instances and their configurations.

- **Specific resources to enumerate:** 
  - List all Compute Engine instances.

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:** 
  - Check if the `shieldedInstanceConfig` key is present for each instance.
  - Verify that both `enableIntegrityMonitoring` and `enableVtpm` are set to `true`.

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:** 
  - Pass: The JSON output must include `shieldedInstanceConfig` with `enableIntegrityMonitoring` and `enableVtpm` both set to `true`.
  - Fail: Absence of `shieldedInstanceConfig` or if either `enableIntegrityMonitoring` or `enableVtpm` is `false`.

- **Example:** 
  Use the command:
  ```bash
  gcloud compute instances list --format=json | jq -r '.[] | select(.shieldedInstanceConfig) | {Name: .name, vTPM: .shieldedInstanceConfig.enableVtpm, IntegrityMonitoring: .shieldedInstanceConfig.enableIntegrityMonitoring}'
  ```
  This command provides a concise view of the relevant properties for each VM instance.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The use of GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands allows for programmatic checks of the Shielded VM settings across all instances within a project.
- The approach leverages the `gcloud compute instances list` command, which is appropriate for enumerating VM instances and their configurations.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly accurate. The use of `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` is valid and provides the necessary information to check the Shielded VM settings.
- The command provided using `jq` is technically correct for filtering and displaying relevant properties.
- The program name `gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_enabled` is descriptive but could be more specific to indicate the exact checks being performed, such as `gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_integrity_vtpm_check`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** Consider renaming to `gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_integrity_vtpm_check` for clarity on what specific aspects of Shielded VM are being verified.
- **Additional Checks:** Ensure that `enableSecureBoot` is also checked as part of the Shielded VM configuration. This is a critical component of Shielded VM security.
- **Alternative Methods:** Consider using the GCP API directly for more extensive automation or integration with other tools. The `instances.get` method in the Compute Engine API can be used to retrieve detailed instance configurations.
- **Output Clarity:** Enhance the output to include the project and zone for each instance, which can be useful for large environments with multiple projects and regions.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-structured and uses appropriate GCP tools and commands. The recommendations for improvement are minor and do not affect the overall validity of the approach.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Modify: Update the program name for clarity and include checks for `enableSecureBoot` in the Shielded VM configuration. This ensures comprehensive verification of all Shielded VM features.
- The automated approach is appropriate and should be maintained, but with the suggested enhancements for completeness and clarity.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The audit of ensuring Compute Instances are launched with Shielded VM enabled can be effectively automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation through the use of `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` to retrieve and verify the Shielded VM configurations. The necessary properties, such as `enableVtpm` and `enableIntegrityMonitoring`, are accessible via the GCP API, making this a suitable candidate for automation.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_integrity_vtpm_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Compute Instances
- API/CLI Method: Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) `compute instances list --format=json`
- Properties to Check: `shieldedInstanceConfig.enableVtpm`, `shieldedInstanceConfig.enableIntegrityMonitoring`
- Pass Criteria: Both `enableVtpm` and `enableIntegrityMonitoring` are set to `true`.
- Fail Criteria: Either `enableVtpm` or `enableIntegrityMonitoring` is set to `false` or the `shieldedInstanceConfig` is absent.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
Both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach, but the second opinion suggested a more descriptive program name and additional checks. The final decision incorporates these suggestions by using a more descriptive program name and ensuring the automation checks for the critical components of Shielded VM.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Cloud_Platform_Foundation_Benchmark_v4_0_0
